intro: The most concrete split between rrweb and Maze is the pricing model: rrweb runs on free and open-source, while Maze uses freemium. That shapes how predictable your monthly spend is and how much of the cost sits in infrastructure versus per-seat or per-event fees. Beyond the headline, rrweb carries low lock-in (oss session replay lib) and free and open-source; Maze carries medium lock-in (research data exportable) and freemium. rrweb is a fit when you are engineers building custom replay, qa or debugging tooling in-house. Maze is a fit when you are ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch. The honest trade-off: rrweb is explicitly not for non-technical teams expecting a hosted dashboard out of the box; Maze is explicitly not for engineering teams looking for production session replay and error triage. On the product side, rrweb is described as Open-source web session replay library. Pixel-perfect recording and playback APIs used by major platforms (PostHog, OpenReplay and many tools), and Maze is described as User research and testing platform for rapid prototype testing, card sorting, tree testing, surveys and.
quick_take: rrweb is for engineers building custom replay, qa or debugging tooling in-house; Maze is for ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose rrweb if you want a fully free, open-source option and can handle self-hosting or self-support.
verdict_b: Choose Maze if its freemium pricing model fits your budget shape better than rrweb's free model, and you are ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch.

faq.a: Direct comparison is hard. rrweb is free and open-source, so licence cost is zero though you pay in operational effort. Maze uses freemium, so it costs money in exchange for managed service. Which is cheaper depends on how much engineering time you value.

faq.q: Can I migrate from rrweb to Maze?
faq.a: Migration from rrweb to Maze is plausible because rrweb has low lock-in (oss session replay lib). You would still have to map config, permissions and integrations to Maze's model. Plan for dual-run and rollback.

On our scoring rrweb rates 5/5 for developer experience versus 3/5 for Maze. That suggests day-to-day workflows (CLI, SDKs, docs) are smoother in rrweb. Scores are directional; run a short spike on your own stack to confirm.

faq.q: Is Maze a good alternative to rrweb?
faq.a: Maze is pitched at ux and design teams validating prototypes and usability pre-launch, which overlaps with rrweb's fit (engineers building custom replay, qa or debugging tooling in-house) but is not identical. Note that Maze is explicitly not for engineering teams looking for production session replay and error triage, so it only replaces rrweb if you are outside that exclusion. The lock-in profile differs (low vs medium), which is the main thing to weigh.
